reasoning: The target to beat for this resolution is that the 2025 fertility rate must be higher than the 2023 baseline of 0.72. Provisional data from Statistics Korea shows that the 2024 fertility rate has already exceeded this threshold, rising to 0.75. This increase was driven by a post-COVID marriage boom, where marriages in 2024 surged by 14.8% — the highest rate of increase on record. Because of the strong lag effect between marriage and childbirth in South Korea (usually 1–2 years), the 2024 marriage surge provides robust structural support for 2025 birth rates. Furthermore, early 2025 metrics confirm this momentum: January 2025 births jumped by 11.6% year-over-year compared to January 2024. With 2025 starting off at a pace far greater than 2024 (let alone 2023), and the 2025 threshold being lower than the 2024 reality, the probability that the 2025 fertility rate stays above 0.72 is exceedingly high. The December 2024 martial law attempt may affect long-term family planning, but given biological realities, its negative impact could not affect early-mid 2025 births, making it very unlikely to drag the full 2025 average back below 0.72.
